text.fullText: The proposed resolution is a cause for concern because of its intention to limit the power of the States and authorized Tribes in protecting the water quality of their designated regions. This is a detrimental decision because these entities are the most knowledgeable on their region's needs and have the resources and vested interests in solving water quality issues as soon as they are detected. Hindering their power will cause more harm to the water and in turn the environment. The specific clause that this resolution is proposing a change to is in the Clean Water Act section 401. Previously this section can be cited by the EPA as defining the responsibilities of the States and authorized Tribes as “No license or permit shall be granted if certification has been denied by the State, interstate agency, or the Administrator, as the case may be” (US EPA). This means that presently if the State does not grant the certification the request will be denied. This upholds the best interests of the rest of the individuals residing in the given region that is being affected. The notion that it is important for the States and authorized Tribes to have a significant say in the ability of licenses or permits being issued can also be seen in examples of case law. A prime example of this can be seen in a case originating in New York. This was known as Constitution Pipeline Co. versus New York State Department of Environmental Conservation. In this instance, Constitution Pipeline tried to argue that the State of New York should not have been able to deny their certification in regards to water quality. However, after hearing their arguments, the courts ultimately decided that they disagreed with Constitution Pipeline Co. Their official ruling was that “we conclude that respondents' actions were within their statutory authority and that the decision was not arbitrary or capricious” (Constitution Pipeline Co 2). This is significant because this case took place recently, occurring in 2017, just under ten years ago. This goes to show that the consensus of the court was recent and still relevant to today. Another important distinction in this case is that Constitution Pipeline Co tried to argue that the State took too long to deliver the decision in rejecting their certification. Essentially, they were arguing that the timeline the State had was too long. The court's response to this was that “to the extent that petitioner challenges the timeliness of respondents' decision, we conclude that we lack jurisdiction over that challenge” (Constitution Pipeline Co 2). This is notable because the new proposed solution is that the State should have a decision within a reasonable amount of time. This is potentially harmful because this applies pressure on the State to grant decisions when they may not be ready to. This goes against ensuring that the most fair decision is reached because States now have a deadline in place for them when they are already limited on resources. This presents an unnecessary strain on the State. This is also a difficult measure to meet given that the proposed amendment does not give a definitive timeline, it only signifies it must be reasonable. The issue of the timeline has already been called into question when there wasn’t a limitation set in place, as was shown in the case. So, if they call for a reasonable turnaround time it is justifiable to assume that the amount of lawsuits surrounding an unfair response time will increase unnecessarily, especially given the vague language used. Another example of needing to maintain the current powers granted to the States can be seen in Jefferson City versus Washington Department of Ecology. This case argued over if it was within Washington’s right to place restrictions on Jefferson City on issues related to water quality. Again, the courts ruled in favor of the state arguing that it was within their means. They stated that “the State Supreme Court affirmed, holding that the antidegradation provisions of the State's water quality standards require the imposition of minimum stream flows, and that § 401 authorized the stream flow condition and conferred on States power to consider all state action related to water quality in imposing conditions on § 401 certificate” (PUD No. 1 of Jefferson Cty 701). Here it can be seen that the courts believed the States should have a say. This further goes to show the importance of having State representation on issues related to their regions because they know it best and have the most interest in maintaining and improving its environment. Ultimately, it is important that the bandwidth of the States not be limited because this will negatively affect the environment. More leniency will be given to organizations that are already difficult to regulate. Works Cited US EPA, OW. “Clean Water Act Section 401: State Certification of Water Quality.” Www.epa.gov, 3 Mar. 2015, www.epa.gov/cwa-401/clean-water-act-section-401-state-certification-water-quality. “Constitution Pipeline Co. V. New York State Department of Environmental Conservation, No. 16-1568 (2d Cir. 2017).” Justia Law, 2017, law.justia.com/cases/federal/appellate-courts/ca2/16-1568/16-1568-2017-08-18.html. Accessed 13 Feb. 2026. “PUD No. 1 of Jefferson Cty. V. Washington Dept. Of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700 (1994).” Justia Law, 2024, supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/511/700/.
